title: Telephony Magazine — 'Early hopes quickly dashed: Post-attack demand for conferencing and hosting services may be fleeting' (Vincent Ryan; PSK Aberdeen CRO quoted, 2001-09-24)
author: Vincent Ryan (Telephony staff)
methodology: Telephony staff feature on whether post-9/11 telecom uplift would prove durable. Quotes PSK as 'chief research officer for Aberdeen Group' on full-motion video economics ($30/hr vs flight) — pre-Zoom era videoconferencing positioning.
abstract: Telephony's Sept 24 2001 cover story examines whether 9/11 demand would actually rescue battered telecom, conferencing, hosting and wireless markets. PSK quoted: 'It costs about $30 an hour for full-motion video. That's dirt cheap compared with a coast-to-coast flight.' Christine Hartman (Probe Research voice-over-packet markets) cautions that changing business communication habits won't be easy. Equinix's Jay Adelson cited on geographic redundancy ('You have to be geographically diverse and go to two IBXs'). Edward Jackson (US Bancorp Piper Jaffray) says telecom impact will be 'negligible' because companies are already trading at 1-1.5x tangible book. Becky Replogle-Wilkes (Sprint collaborative apps marketing) endorses the conferencing case. Polycom/PictureTel merger DOJ-approved cited as videoconferencing-sector indicator. NEON Communications OC-12 fiber set up at 60 Hudson St. for NYC City Hall as backup-carrier example.
importance_rationale: PSK as Aberdeen CRO with one of the earliest archival-record videoconferencing-economics quotes — anchors 'pre-Zoom era full-motion video at $30/hr' as a pricing data point.
relevance_rationale: Confirms PSK's CRO title at Aberdeen by Sept 2001 (consistent with the April 2002 WatchIT presenter byline in Study 3 above and the Nov 2001 M&C quote in Study 10 below).
prescience_rationale: PSK's Sept 2001 framing of full-motion video at $30/hr vs a coast-to-coast flight is an early articulation of the eventual Zoom-era thesis. Adelson's geographic-redundancy framing anticipates the multi-region cloud and CDN era. The Polycom/PictureTel merger consolidated the room-system videoconferencing market that Zoom and Microsoft Teams ultimately disrupted ~2018-2020. Hartman's habit-change caution proved correct in the short term (videoconferencing did not break out post-9/11) but 2020-era pandemic conditions broke the habit barrier 19 years later.
